*Baseline orchestration skill on remote platform and SRE roles.*

Kubernetes is now baseline on remote platform and SRE postings. The differentiator at senior levels is multi-cluster, multi-region operations experience.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/kubernetes

---

## Docker (devops)

*Ubiquitous container tooling on remote engineering postings.*

Docker is ubiquitous on remote engineering listings. Container literacy is now an entry-level expectation, not a senior differentiator.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/docker

---

## Terraform (devops)

*Primary IaC tool on remote platform postings.*

Terraform appears on nearly all remote platform engineering postings as the infrastructure-as-code tool. OpenTofu adoption is rising on more security-conscious teams.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/terraform

---

## Ansible (devops)

*Common configuration management tool on remote ops roles.*

Ansible appears on remote ops postings that manage long-running VM fleets and on hybrid cloud environments.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/ansible

---

## PostgreSQL (data)

*Default OLTP database across remote backend postings.*

PostgreSQL is the default OLTP database in remote postings. Knowledge of advanced features (logical replication, partial indexes, RLS) marks senior listings.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/postgres

---

## MongoDB (data)

*Common document store on remote backend postings.*

MongoDB demand is steady but smaller than five years ago. New listings often pair it with caching layers and event streaming.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/mongodb

---

## MySQL (data)

*Widespread on remote backend postings at established companies.*

MySQL and MariaDB demand on remote postings is steady, particularly at established consumer SaaS and at content-heavy platforms.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/mysql

---

## Redis (data)

*Ubiquitous caching and queuing primitive on remote backend roles.*

Redis appears on most remote backend postings as the caching, session and lightweight queueing primitive.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/redis

---

## Elasticsearch (data)

*Search and log analytics skill across remote backend postings.*

Elasticsearch and its OpenSearch fork are commonly listed on remote backend postings for search, logging and analytics workloads.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/elasticsearch

---

## Kafka (data)

*Premium event streaming skill on remote data platform postings.*

Kafka shows up on remote data platform and event-driven backend postings. Senior remote postings name it as a hard requirement for distributed systems roles.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/kafka

---

## Snowflake (data)

*Leading data warehouse on remote data engineering postings.*

Snowflake leads the remote data warehouse listings. Knowledge of cost optimization and access control patterns is the senior differentiator.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/snowflake

---

## BigQuery (data)

*Default warehouse on remote GCP-anchored data teams.*

BigQuery anchors remote data engineering listings at GCP-centric companies. Familiarity with slot management and BI Engine is the senior signal.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/bigquery

---

## dbt (data)

*Default transformation layer on remote analytics engineering roles.*

dbt is now ubiquitous on remote analytics engineering postings. Most postings expect both Core and Cloud familiarity.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/dbt

---

## Apache Spark (data)

*Distributed processing skill on remote data engineering roles.*

Spark and PySpark remain dominant on remote data engineering postings dealing with large-scale batch and streaming pipelines.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/spark

---

## Apache Airflow (data)

*Most-listed orchestrator on remote data engineering roles.*

Airflow is the most-listed workflow orchestrator on remote data engineering postings. Prefect and Dagster appear on a growing minority of newer listings.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/airflow

---

## PyTorch (ai)

*Default ML framework on remote ML engineering postings.*

PyTorch is the dominant deep learning framework on remote ML engineering postings since 2022. Familiarity with distributed training and serving is the senior signal.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/pytorch

---

## TensorFlow (ai)

*Established ML framework on remote ML engineering postings.*

TensorFlow demand remains on remote ML postings, particularly at enterprise companies maintaining established production pipelines.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/tensorflow

---

## LLM Engineering (ai)

*The fastest-growing remote AI specialty since 2023.*

LLM engineering listings on remote postings have grown sharply since 2023. Production experience with retrieval-augmented generation, fine-tuning and evals is the senior differentiator. Salaries on dedicated LLM engineering postings sit at 180 to 280 thousand USD remotely at well-funded companies.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/llm

---

## RAG (ai)

*Production retrieval skill across remote AI engineering postings.*

Retrieval-augmented generation experience is named on most remote AI engineering postings since 2024. Vector database familiarity and embedding choice tradeoffs are typical interview topics.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/rag

---

## Hugging Face (ai)

*Default ML model platform on remote ML engineering postings.*

Hugging Face Transformers, Datasets and Hub appear on most remote ML engineering postings. Model fine-tuning experience using their stack is a common requirement.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/huggingface

---

## GraphQL (web3)

*API layer skill on remote full-stack postings.*

GraphQL remains on a meaningful minority of remote full-stack postings. Apollo and urql are the most-named clients.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/graphql

---

## Tailwind CSS (framework)

*Default CSS framework on modern remote front-end roles.*

Tailwind CSS is now the default styling system on most new remote front-end postings, often paired with shadcn or other component libraries.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/tailwind

---

## Redux (framework)

*Established React state management on remote front-end roles.*

Redux and Redux Toolkit appear on a meaningful share of remote React postings, particularly at established product teams maintaining mature applications.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/redux

---

## Flutter (mobile)

*Primary cross-platform mobile skill on remote mobile postings.*

Flutter dominates the cross-platform mobile category on remote postings, ahead of React Native at most of the newer listings since 2023.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/flutter

---

## React Native (mobile)

*Established cross-platform mobile skill on remote postings.*

React Native remains a strong demand on remote mobile postings, particularly at companies whose web stack is React.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/react-native

---

## Unity (framework)

*Primary game engine on remote gameplay engineering postings.*

Unity remains the dominant engine on remote gameplay engineering postings at indie and mid-size studios.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/unity

---

## Unreal Engine (framework)

*AAA-tier engine on remote gameplay engineering postings.*

Unreal Engine 5 listings on remote postings are smaller in count but pay among the highest bands for gameplay engineers.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/unreal

---

## Solidity (web3)

*Primary smart contract language on remote crypto postings.*

Solidity remains the primary smart contract language on remote crypto and DeFi postings. Audit-grade experience commands the highest bands in the category.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/solidity

---

## Cybersecurity (devops)

*Premium-paid remote category with shortage of senior candidates.*

Remote cybersecurity postings remain underserved by candidates at senior levels. AppSec, cloud security and detection engineering are the most active sub-categories on remote postings.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/cybersecurity

---

## Figma (framework)

*Universal design tool on remote product design postings.*

Figma is universally listed on remote product design postings. Design system maintenance experience is the senior signal.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/figma

---

## Storybook (framework)

*Common UI component documentation tool on remote front-end roles.*

Storybook appears on most remote front-end postings that mention a design system or component library.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/storybook

---

## CI/CD (devops)

*Pipeline skills required across remote engineering postings.*

CI/CD pipeline literacy is now a baseline expectation on remote engineering postings. GitHub Actions is the most-listed tool, with GitLab CI second.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/cicd

---

## SQL (data)

*Foundational data skill on remote analytics, data and backend roles.*

SQL remains the foundational data skill on remote postings across backend, data and analytics. Window functions and CTEs are now expected at all levels.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/sql

---

## Machine Learning (ai)

*Broadest AI category on remote engineering postings.*

Machine learning is the broadest AI tag on remote postings. Generalist ML engineering and applied ML on production systems dominate the listings.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/machine-learning

---

## Data Engineering (data)

*Premium-paid remote data category with steady demand.*

Data engineering postings on remote listings cluster around pipeline ownership, warehouse modeling and platform reliability. Snowflake, dbt and Airflow appear together on most listings.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/data-engineering

---

## Prompt Engineering (ai)

*Specialized AI skill peaking on remote applied AI postings.*

Dedicated prompt engineering postings remain a small share, with most demand absorbed into broader AI and LLM engineering roles. The skill is still explicitly named on a meaningful minority of remote AI listings.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/prompt-engineering

---

## WebGL (framework)

*Specialized graphics skill on remote front-end and 3D postings.*

WebGL and Three.js appear on a minority of remote front-end postings, with demand concentrated at 3D product configurators, design tools and game studios.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/skills/webgl

---

# Comparisons

## Remote vs hybrid work in tech

*The differences that matter when choosing between fully remote and hybrid roles.*

## Geographic flexibility
Fully remote roles allow choosing any compatible-timezone city. Hybrid roles tie residence to commuting distance.

## Compensation
Fully remote bands at well-funded companies sit within 10 to 15 percent of in-office equivalents. Hybrid roles typically match in-office bands.

## Career velocity
Hybrid roles still produce faster promotion velocity at most companies, though the gap has narrowed since 2022. The mechanism is informal visibility rather than explicit policy.

## Team integration
Hybrid teams reach informal alignment faster. Fully remote teams require deliberate process to match this.

## Choice in 2026
The choice now depends less on availability (both are abundant) and more on lifestyle and career stage preferences.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/compare/remote-vs-hybrid

---

## Remote vs onsite tech jobs

*The trade-offs between fully remote and fully onsite tech employment.*

## Geographic flexibility
Remote removes the geographic constraint entirely. Onsite locks residence to commute distance.

## Compensation
At equivalent companies and seniority, onsite bands run slightly higher (typically 5 to 15 percent). The gap has narrowed since 2022.

## Career development
Onsite roles still confer mentorship and informal learning advantages, particularly early-career. The gap is smallest at remote-mature companies.

## Lifestyle
Remote removes commute time. Onsite provides structural separation between work and home.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/compare/remote-vs-onsite

---

## Freelance vs full-time remote tech work

*How the freelance and full-time tracks differ for remote tech workers.*

## Income
Freelance day rates are typically 1.5 to 2x the equivalent annual full-time rate divided by working days. Variance is much higher.

## Benefits
Full-time roles include health insurance, retirement and equity. These narrow the apparent income gap materially.

## Project depth
Full-time roles allow deeper ownership of long-running systems. Freelance roles tend toward shorter scoped engagements.

## Tax
Freelance allows business expense deductions but typically pays more in social charges.

## Time to first contract vs first job
Freelance contracts can convert faster initially but compound more slowly. Full-time roles compound career capital faster at established employers.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/compare/freelance-vs-fulltime

---

## US vs EU remote tech companies

*How US-headquartered and EU-headquartered remote tech employers compare on compensation, culture, and operations.*

## Compensation
US-headquartered remote tech employers pay higher cash bands at equivalent seniority, typically 30 to 60 percent above EU equivalents in raw dollars.

## Benefits
EU employers provide more statutory benefits (paid vacation, healthcare, parental leave) that narrow the total compensation gap meaningfully.

## Vacation
US companies typically offer 15 to 25 days; EU companies 25 to 35 days plus statutory holidays.

## Work culture
US remote-first companies often run faster cadences with longer working hours. EU companies typically enforce stricter working hours norms.

## Timezone fit
US employers generally hire candidates with at least 4 hours of US timezone overlap; EU employers more flexible on candidate timezone.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/compare/us-vs-eu-remote-tech-companies

---

## Junior vs senior remote tech jobs

*How remote hiring differs by experience level in 2026.*

## Availability
Senior remote roles outnumber junior remote roles substantially. Most fully remote teams hire juniors only when they have a structured mentorship program.

## Compensation
Junior remote roles cluster at 70 to 110 thousand USD. Senior at 150 to 220. Staff and principal at 220 to 320.

## Hiring bar
Junior remote hiring places more weight on portfolio quality and async communication, since onboarding investment is higher.

## Path
Many remote engineers start their careers onsite or hybrid, then transition to fully remote at mid-level.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/compare/junior-vs-senior-remote-jobs

---

## React vs Vue remote job market

*How React and Vue compare on remote tech postings in 2026.*

## Volume
React listings outnumber Vue listings on remote postings by roughly 4 to 1.

## Geographic distribution
Vue has stronger relative representation on EU postings, particularly in Germany, Netherlands and France.

## Salary
At equivalent seniority and company size, salary bands are similar.

## Career mobility
React fluency provides more job options. Vue fluency is highly valued at the smaller pool of teams using it.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/compare/react-vs-vue-jobs

---

## Python vs Go for remote backend work

*How Python and Go compare on remote backend postings in 2026.*

## Volume
Python backend listings outnumber Go listings by roughly 3 to 1.

## Domain
Python dominates data, ML and content-heavy backends. Go dominates platform engineering, infrastructure and high-throughput services.

## Salary
Go listings pay a modest premium (roughly 10 to 15 percent at senior levels) reflecting smaller talent pool and higher complexity domains.

## Learning curve
Go has a flatter learning curve to production-readiness. Python has a flatter learning curve to first hire.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/compare/python-vs-go-backend

---

## Startup vs big tech remote engineering

*How startup and large-company remote engineering differ in 2026.*

## Compensation
Big tech still pays higher cash and clearer equity at senior levels. Startups pay variable cash with higher upside (and higher variance) equity.

## Scope
Startup engineers ship across the stack with more autonomy. Big tech engineers go deeper on narrower problems.

## Career velocity
Startup roles can produce faster level-up through scope expansion. Big tech offers more structured progression.

## Stability
Big tech offers materially more income and employment stability.

## Remote culture
Many big tech companies have moved to hybrid since 2023. Most truly remote-first companies are at the startup to scale-up size.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/compare/startup-vs-bigtech-remote

---

## AWS vs GCP remote job demand

*Comparing the major cloud platforms by remote hiring demand in 2026.*

## Volume
AWS appears on roughly 60 percent of remote backend and platform postings. GCP on roughly 30 percent. Postings naming both are common.

## Domain
GCP has stronger relative representation in data engineering and ML postings, driven by BigQuery and Vertex AI adoption.

## Salary
Equivalent cloud certifications carry similar weight. AWS Solutions Architect Professional and GCP Professional Cloud Architect both register as senior signals.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/compare/aws-vs-gcp-remote-jobs

---

## Snowflake vs BigQuery for remote data engineers

*How the leading cloud data warehouses split on remote data engineering postings.*

## Volume
Snowflake leads Snowflake-or-BigQuery listings on remote postings by a slight margin. Many listings name both.

## Stack alignment
Snowflake postings cluster at AWS-anchored stacks. BigQuery dominates GCP-anchored stacks.

## Skills
dbt experience is universal across both. SQL window function fluency is universal.

## Salary
Equivalent at senior data engineering levels.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/compare/snowflake-vs-bigquery

---

## Employer of record vs direct employment for remote tech workers

*When to use an employer of record service versus direct employment.*

## Use case
EOR services let foreign companies employ workers in countries where they have no legal entity. Direct employment requires the employer to have local presence.

## Cost
EOR adds a per-employee monthly fee, typically 400 to 900 USD. Borne by the employer in most cases.

## Tax and social charges
EOR handles local payroll, tax withholding and social contributions. The worker receives clean local payslips.

## Benefits
EOR-employed workers receive statutory local benefits. Equity and stock options are harder to structure through EOR.

## Choice
Direct employment is cleaner where the employer has local presence. EOR is the standard solution for international remote hires at smaller companies.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/compare/employer-of-record-vs-direct

---

## In-house vs agency remote tech roles

*How agency and in-house remote engineering compare.*

## Project variety
Agency engineers see more projects per year. In-house engineers see deeper engagement with fewer systems.

## Compensation
In-house product teams typically pay 10 to 20 percent more for equivalent seniority. Agencies pay variable amounts including utilization bonuses.

## Career capital
In-house ownership of long-running systems compounds more clearly into senior engineering credentials. Agency variety compounds into a different skill set centered on adapting to constraints quickly.

## Remote fit
Both can work fully remote. Agencies have been earlier and more thorough adopters of remote-first operations at most established names.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/compare/in-house-vs-agency-remote

---

## Lisbon vs Barcelona for remote tech workers

*How the two leading Mediterranean remote tech hubs actually compare.*

## Visa
Portugal D8 and Spain DNV are roughly equivalent in friction. Portugal’s longer-term tax pathway is more clearly defined post-NHR reforms.

## Cost of living
Lisbon central rents have risen sharply since 2022, partially closing the gap with Barcelona. Barcelona remains 10 to 20 percent more expensive for equivalent apartments.

## Tech ecosystem
Lisbon’s ecosystem has grown faster since 2020. Barcelona retains a larger local employer base but a smaller share of remote-first companies.

## Lifestyle
Lisbon: smaller, more walkable, English-friendly. Barcelona: bigger, more international, more nightlife and culture.

## Choice
For a first move to Mediterranean remote life: Lisbon. For a base with more depth and Spanish exposure: Barcelona.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/compare/lisbon-vs-barcelona-remote

---

## Portugal D8 vs Spain Digital Nomad Visa

*Side-by-side comparison of the two leading EU nomad visa programs.*

## Income threshold
Portugal: ~3 280 EUR/month. Spain: ~2 760 EUR/month.

## Tax regime
Portugal’s NHR successor offers preferential treatment for specific high-value activities including most software engineering. Spain’s Beckham Law extension caps Spanish-source income at 24 percent flat for six years.

## Processing time
Portugal: 60 to 90 days from consulate to arrival. Spain: 20 days for in-country applications.

## Path to permanent residency
Portugal: 5 years to permanent, 5 years to citizenship. Spain: 5 years to permanent, 10 years to citizenship.

## Family
Both allow dependents. Spain processes spouse applications slightly faster in 2026.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/compare/portugal-vs-spain-nomad-visa

---

## Lisbon vs Mexico City for remote tech workers

*European versus Latin American remote tech hub: timezone is the deciding factor.*

## Timezone overlap with US employers
Lisbon: 3 to 4 hours of US East Coast overlap. Mexico City: 9 hours of full overlap with US Central.

## Cost of living
Mexico City is roughly 25 percent cheaper than Lisbon for equivalent neighborhoods (Roma/Condesa vs Principe Real).

## Lifestyle
Lisbon: European city scale, ocean nearby, very walkable. Mexico City: massive metro, food scene unmatched, urban density different from European norms.

## Visa
Portugal D8 vs Mexico Temporary Resident. Both are straightforward for engineers with stable remote income.

## Choice
US employers + lower cost: Mexico City. EU employers or eventual EU residency: Lisbon.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/compare/lisbon-vs-mexico-city-remote

---

## Bali vs Chiang Mai for remote tech workers

*The two longest-running Southeast Asian remote work hubs compared.*

## Lifestyle
Bali: beach plus jungle, larger and louder community, more partying. Chiang Mai: smaller city, mountains, slower pace, more long-term residents.

## Cost
Chiang Mai is roughly 30 percent cheaper than Bali on like-for-like accommodations and food.

## Visa
Indonesia: B211a visit visa, Second Home Visa for longer. Thailand: DTV (Destination Thailand Visa) since 2024, valid 5 years multi-entry.

## Internet
Bali Canggu: 100 to 300 Mbps depending on neighborhood. Chiang Mai: consistent 150 to 200 Mbps citywide.

## Community
Bali has more nomad coliving infrastructure. Chiang Mai has a longer-established and more stable community.

## Choice
Younger or shorter-term: Bali. Longer-term or quieter: Chiang Mai.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/compare/bali-vs-chiang-mai-remote

---

# Glossary

## Async (Asynchronous communication)

Communicating without expecting real-time replies.

Default working pattern of mature remote teams. Decisions written down, discussions happening across hours not minutes, video calls reserved for irreducibly synchronous work.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#async

## RTO (Return To Office)

Corporate policy bringing employees back to in-person work.

Most large US tech employers announced RTO between 2022 and 2024, typically requiring three days a week on site. Most remote-first companies have not.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#rto

## WFH (Work From Home)

Working remotely from a residential setting.

Often used interchangeably with remote work, though WFH technically excludes travel-based remote (digital nomad) setups.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#wfh

## Remote-first

Company where remote is the default operating mode.

Distinct from remote-friendly: remote-first companies design processes, tooling, and culture around distributed work. Documentation is mandatory, meetings are exceptional, hiring is geography-agnostic.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#remote-first

## Remote-friendly

Company that allows remote work but defaults to in-office.

Many remote-friendly companies provide a worse remote experience than remote-first peers because culture and tooling still privilege those in the office.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#remote-friendly

## Hybrid work

Mix of remote and in-office days.

Typically one to three days in office per week. The dominant pattern at most large tech employers in 2026 outside of remote-first scale-ups.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#hybrid

## Distributed team

Team whose members live in multiple cities or countries.

Distributed is broader than remote: a distributed team may have multiple physical offices in different regions, while a remote team typically has no office at all.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#distributed-team

## Timezone overlap

Hours when two distributed teammates are both working.

The single most filtered criterion on remote engineering postings in 2026. Four hours of overlap is the working minimum for most synchronous-leaning roles; two hours works for IC-deep work.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#timezone-overlap

## EOR (Employer of Record)

Third-party that legally employs a worker on behalf of a foreign company.

Common solution when a US-based company wants to hire an EU resident. The EOR (Deel, Remote.com, Oyster) handles local payroll, tax, and compliance. Typically costs the employer 400 to 900 USD per month per employee.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#eor

## IC (Individual Contributor)

Engineer who builds, distinct from a manager.

The IC track typically extends from junior through senior, staff, principal, and distinguished levels. Many remote-first companies favor strong IC ladders over management progression.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#ic

## PIP (Performance Improvement Plan)

Formal documented underperformance process.

Usually 30 to 90 days. In remote contexts, almost always precedes termination. Treated as termination notice at most US employers.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#pip

## RSU (Restricted Stock Unit)

Company stock granted as part of compensation, vesting over time.

Standard equity vehicle at public tech companies. Vests over four years with a one-year cliff typically. Taxed as ordinary income on vest at most jurisdictions.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#rsu

## ESOP (Employee Stock Ownership Plan)

Pool of company shares reserved for employees.

At private companies, employee stock options are granted from the ESOP pool. Total ESOP typically sits between 10 and 20 percent of company shares at well-structured startups.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#esop

## DNV (Digital Nomad Visa)

Visa category specifically for remote workers employed abroad.

Roughly 50 countries had a dedicated DNV by 2026. Portugal D8, Spain DNV, Greece DNV, Estonia DNV and Mexico Temporary Resident are the most-used by tech workers.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#dnv

## Geo-arbitrage

Earning in a high-income currency while spending in a low-cost location.

Common practice among remote workers earning USD or EUR while living in lower-cost regions. Real wealth-builder over multi-year horizons but creates compliance complexity (tax residency, social charges).

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#geo-arbitrage

## Permanent establishment

Tax concept where a company is considered to have a taxable presence in a country.

Risk for employers of remote workers: if an employee performs core business functions in a country where the company has no entity, tax authorities may declare the company has permanent establishment there. EOR services exist primarily to avoid this.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#permanent-establishment

## Right to disconnect

Legal protection against work communications outside working hours.

Enacted in France, Portugal, Spain, Italy, Australia and Ontario. Generally requires employers to respect employee off-hours and to formalize working hour expectations in writing.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#right-to-disconnect

## OKR (Objectives and Key Results)

Goal-setting framework for measurable team outputs.

Dominant goal-setting framework at remote tech companies. Quarterly cadence with three to five objectives, each broken into measurable key results.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#okr

## Standup

Recurring team sync, usually daily.

Remote teams typically run async written standups in a shared channel. Synchronous video standups persist but have lost ground since 2022.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#standup

## Sprint

Time-boxed development cycle, typically two weeks.

Remote teams often run two-week sprints with planning at the start and a retrospective at the end. Asynchronous sprint planning has emerged as a viable alternative to the traditional synchronous meeting.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#sprint

## Retrospective

Team review of what worked and what did not.

Typically at the end of each sprint. Remote teams often run async retros via collaborative documents like Miro or shared docs, with synchronous review only of the discussion items.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#retro

## ATS (Applicant Tracking System)

Software that companies use to manage candidate pipelines.

Greenhouse, Lever and Workday dominate. The actual job listings on most company career sites are powered by an ATS. Aggregators like slateremote.com pull from these ATS APIs.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#ats

## Severance

Payment made when an employee is terminated without cause.

Varies enormously: at-will US employment provides no statutory severance; most EU countries mandate at least 1 month per year of service. Negotiating severance at signing is rare but high-value at senior levels.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#severance

## Notice period

Mandatory advance warning before quitting or being terminated.

Two weeks is standard at US employers. Most EU jurisdictions mandate one to three months. Notice periods are negotiable at signing in many countries.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#notice-period

## Non-compete

Clause preventing an employee from joining competitors for a period after leaving.

Banned in California and many EU countries. Enforceable but limited in most US states. Increasingly rare and weakly enforceable in tech in 2026.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#non-compete

## NDA (Non-Disclosure Agreement)

Confidentiality obligation about company information.

Universal at tech employers. NDAs covering interview content (during hiring) are common and legally enforceable.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#nda

## Founding engineer

Early engineering hire at a startup, typically employees 1 to 10.

Implies significantly higher equity (0.5 to 2 percent typically) and broader scope than later hires. Most founding engineering roles are not strictly remote even at remote-friendly startups.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#founding-engineer

## Staff engineer

Senior IC level above senior engineer, below principal.

Roughly 8 to 12 years of experience equivalent. Compensation bands sit at 220 to 320 thousand USD at well-funded remote employers. Significant scope of technical influence beyond individual code.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#staff-engineer

## Principal engineer

Senior IC level above staff.

Roughly 12 to 20 years of experience equivalent. Often spans multiple teams. Compensation bands at 280 to 450 thousand USD at well-funded remote employers.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#principal-engineer

## 10x engineer

Cliché for an engineer perceived as ten times more productive than the median.

Largely discredited as a concept. The reality is that engineering effectiveness depends on context, codebase familiarity, and team dynamics far more than individual heroics.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#ten-x

## Burnout

Chronic workplace stress that has not been successfully managed.

In remote contexts, burnout often manifests as isolation rather than overwork. The combination of lack of separation between work and home plus reduced social signals is the most common driver.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#burnout

## Layoff

Termination not based on individual performance.

Tech layoffs hit a multi-year peak in 2023 to 2024 and stabilized by 2026. Severance packages at major employers typically run 2 to 6 months of base salary plus accelerated equity vesting.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#layoff

## PTO (Paid Time Off)

Bank of paid vacation days.

US norm is 15 to 25 days. EU statutory minimums range from 20 to 30 days plus public holidays. Most remote-first companies offer "unlimited PTO" which in practice averages closer to the US norm than the EU norm.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#pto

## CV vs Resume

Different naming conventions for the application document.

Resume is US/Canada norm: one page, sometimes two. CV is EU/UK norm: longer, more detail. For remote applications to US companies, default to the US resume convention.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#cv

## T-shape engineer

Broad knowledge across many areas plus deep expertise in one.

The dominant profile that remote engineering teams value: breadth to communicate across boundaries plus depth to ship in one area.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#t-shape

## MVP (Minimum Viable Product)

Smallest version of a product that delivers value to users.

Used loosely. In practice, most "MVPs" ship with more scope than the term implies. The valuable usage is to constrain scope early in a project.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#mvp

## TDD (Test-Driven Development)

Writing tests before code.

Less dogmatic than a decade ago. Most senior engineers practice test-first selectively, particularly for complex logic or external interfaces.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#tdd

## CI/CD (Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment)

Automated build, test, and deployment pipelines.

Universal expectation on remote engineering roles. GitHub Actions is the most-listed tool, with GitLab CI second.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#ci-cd

## Slowmad / Slowmadism

Digital nomad lifestyle with longer stays in each location.

Reaction against fast nomadism (changing cities every few weeks). Slowmads spend three to twelve months per location, integrate more locally, and report better long-term wellbeing.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#slowmad

## Coworking space

Shared workspace open to non-employees of a single company.

WeWork, Selina, Outsite and many local players dominate. Daily passes typically cost 15 to 35 USD; monthly memberships 200 to 500. Most remote workers split time between home and coworking weekly.

Source: https://slateremote.com/en/glossary#coworking
